old_claim.note: Lasers is modeled as a necessary component or method for this technology in the current graph.

new_claim.note: The 1960 anchor used television cloud-cover pictures and image recording from orbit, making photographic imaging lineage an enabling predecessor.

source_shape.source_locator: NOAA says TIROS-1 carried two television cameras and two video recorders, provided forecasters their first view of cloud formations from orbit, and returned more than 19,000 usable pictures.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source-backed 1960 anchor is orbital imaging with television cameras and recorded pictures, not laser optics.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Photography is a defensible image-capture predecessor for TIROS-style orbital imaging, while lasers were invented in 1960 and are not required by this early spacecraft payload.

ontology_before: Space-Based Advanced Optics depended directly on lasers, implying laser components were necessary for the first spaceborne optical systems.
ontology_after: Spaceborne Optical Instruments depends on photographic/television imaging lineage and leaves lasers for later ranging, communication, adaptive-optics, or specialized payload nodes.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: advanced_optics_space_based now depends on photography and no longer directly depends on lasers.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed specifically to laser altimetry, lidar, laser communications, or laser guide-star adaptive optics.
A stronger source shows the 1960 spaceborne optical anchor required an onboard laser.
A separate spaceborne_laser_optics node is added and should carry the laser dependency.
short_reason: The 1960 anchor used television imaging, not lasers.
